comments.text: As a small example: $10588881419, 13604651997$ are two integers which can be expressed in at least $5$ ways as the sum of $3$ cubes of primes (they may be minimal). Creating an OEIS sequence containing the smallest number for which there are at least $r$ possibilities to write it this way, seems already hard (if one wants a decent amount of terms). As primes behave random, there seems no good way to efficiently find those numbers and one likely has to check too many combinations.

Just to check, are you restricting to only positive cubes of primes here? (I assume so, and this must certainly have been Erdős' intent, but occasionaly some radical freethinkers include negative numbers in the set of primes...) Presumably this is 5 'genuinely distinct' different ways?

Substracting prime powers would give a completely different answer.... But so checked about different sets of 3 primes For n=10588881419 (smallest example, now confirmed), the following triples (p,q,r) work for $n=p^3+q^3+r^3$: {59, 1669, 1811},{83, 1567, 1889}{139, 1427, 1973}, {349, 1091, 2099}, {479, 929, 2131}
Yes, of course, that would be a very different answer (and question!), just wanted to make sure. I think that this would make a nice OEIS sequence (5 is enough terms to qualify I believe).
Anyone an idea how the $k=3$ case may have been settled?

Erdős [Er65b] wrote precisely 'I can also prove [the case $k=3$] (unpublished), and the proof of this seems to need some special properties of primes.' Which doesn't give a lot of clues. It is of course possible that Erdős' proof also had a flaw, which he later discovered. One idea is to adapt Mahler's disproof of Hypothesis K for $k=3$ (see this exposition for example). This relies on the fact that, if $n=m^{12}$, then\[ n = (9t^4)^3 + (m^4-9mt^3)^3 + (9m^3t-9t^4)^3.\]In particular, choosing $t\ll m$ sufficiently small gives $\gg m=n^{1/12}$ many ways to write $n$ as the sum of three positive cubes. So one approach would be to give a similar parametric family of solutions (for some $n$ of a special form) and then use sieve theory to show that the forms are simultaneously prime infinitely often. This looks hard, since we know very little about how to find primes among the values of polynomials of degree $>2$, but perhaps one can take advantage of a large number of variables and averaging to make this work.

It might well be that the answer is yes at the end. (average behaviour and extremal behaviour can be expected to be very different) Below some heuristic thinking (not made precise, since proving it in the context will be still hard) Notice there are at least $(\pi(\sqrt[k]{N/k}))^k =\tilde \Omega_k (N)$ and at most $(\pi(\sqrt[k]{N}))^k =\tilde O_k (N)$ many combinations of primes whose sum of k^th powers is bounded by $N.$ Now, if this is somewhat random distributed (it is not uniform random, but should be good for heuristic estimate since one can check $[N/2,N]$..), then one can think about the Birthday paradox for $m$ collisions. See e.g. [oeis/A225852] and [oeis/A225871]. Rough estimates, tell that for every bound on $m$, the critical region should be at some power of $N$ with exponent less than $1$ (similar to [stackexchange]). Since $\tilde\Theta_k(N)$ is linear up to polylog, one expects indeed there are values which appear at least $m$ times...

This should be linked to [322], which asks a similar question for the set of all integers (or for sets of positive density). This is true for the set of all integers when $k=3$, which is surprising for the same heuristic reasons Stijn mentions.

I'm sorry to say, but I don't think your argument can be correct. It is generally assumed that eg $a^5 + b^5 = c^5 + d^5$ has no non-trivial solutions in positive integers, let alone in primes. See for example problem #324.

There is written "One would expect this result to be true for density reasons". Actually, when thinking about the problem, I had exactly the opposite thought. Up to $N^k$, there are less than $\pi(N)^k<<N^k$ many options for the $k$-tuple of primes. So on average, a number in $[0,N^k]$ is almost never hit, i.e., $\mathbb E[ p_k(n)] \to 0$ ($n$ taken at random in large interval). [Let alone, when one considers the sum of only two $k$-powers, then for $k>4$ one may expect no different solutions, as commented by Wouter]
